summary: Gruber pays tribute to Rolling Stones drummer Charlie Watts, who died at age 80. He reflects on his lifelong love of the Stones and Watts's universally acclaimed talent and central role in the band's sound. He notes how the surviving band members — Jagger, Richards, and Wood — were nearly speechless in their grief, sharing simple tributes on Instagram. Gruber also observes that the band's homepage was reduced to a single portrait, reminiscent of Apple's tribute when Steve Jobs died. The post is a rare personal, non-tech reflection from Gruber, touching on music, loss, and the occasional value of social media.
key_points: Charlie Watts was universally praised by musicians who understood rock-and-roll mechanics as essential to the Stones' sound
The surviving Stones members' near-wordless Instagram tributes conveyed the depth of their loss
Gruber draws a parallel between the Rolling Stones homepage tribute and Apple's response to Steve Jobs's death
Instagram, despite its problems, remains the major social platform best suited for sharing genuine positive sentiment
money_quotes: The first time I can remember being asked my favorite band, my answer was The Stones. 40-some years later, my answer is unchanged.

I've never heard a musician who does understand the mechanics of playing rock-and-roll do anything but positively rave about Charlie Watts's talent, and his central role in the Stones' sound and success.
He was also the best-dressed man in all of rock and roll.
Mick Jagger — one of the most poetic, eloquent writers the world has known — had no words.
The band's homepage is just a lovely portrait — reminiscent of what Apple did a decade ago when Steve Jobs died.
I slag on social media frequently, and though Instagram has its problems, when it works, it works.
It's a mere token, but it feels good to press Like on posts like these, just to express, in some small way, that we miss Charlie too.
key_insight: The most fitting tributes to irreplaceable people are often the simplest — a portrait, a photo, a pressed Like button — because some losses leave even the most eloquent without words.
